Standard Report Structure: Line graphs typically show how one or more variables change over time (e.g., years, months). In the introduction, paraphrase the task statement (what is measured, where, when) in 1–2 sentences without copying the rubric. The overview should summarise themain trendssuch as overall increases/decreases, stability, fluctuations, and notable highs or lows, without detailed numbers. One or two detail paragraphs then group data logically (e.g., groups of lines with similar patterns, early period vs later period) and support the overview by selecting and reporting key figures and comparisons.
Tips to Achieve Band 7 and Above: For Band 7 Task Achievement, you must present aclear overview of main trendsand select key features rather than describing every data point. Group lines that behave similarly and compare them using appropriate comparative language (e.g., “in contrast”, “by comparison”, “slightly higher than”). Use a range of vocabulary for describing trends and changes (“rose sharply”, “declined steadily”, “remained relatively stable”) and avoid repetitive use of “increase”/“decrease”. Ensure coherence by organising your detail paragraphs around logical groupings (e.g., highest vs lowest, early vs late years) and using cohesive devices flexibly but not excessively. Grammatically, show control over tenses (usually past or mixed with future for projections), adverbials of degree, and complex sentences expressing comparison and contrast.
Common Mistakes to Avoid: Recounting data mechanically year by year with no overview, which keeps Task Achievement around Band 5–6. Describing every minor fluctuation instead of focusing on significant patterns and key comparisons. Misreading the axes or units (e.g., confusing percentages with actual numbers) and therefore misreporting figures. Overusing memorised trend phrases in a formulaic way, leading to awkward or inaccurate collocations. Using the wrong tense for historical data or mixing tenses inconsistently in the same description. Including explanations, reasons or predictions that are not in the graph, which introduces inappropriate opinion or speculation.

Question Type: Bar chart(categorical comparison over time or at one time)
Standard Report Structure: Bar charts may show comparisons across categories at one time or over time. The introduction paraphrases the prompt (what, where, when, units) in one sentence. The overview identifies the main comparisons: which categories are highest/lowest, any clear ranking, and broad changes if time is included. In the detail paragraphs, group related categories (e.g., those with similar values, or top vs bottom groups) and report key numbers and contrasts in 1–2 logically ordered body paragraphs.
Tips to Achieve Band 7 and Above: High‑band answers avoid listing bars individually and insteadcompare groups and extremes. For Task Achievement at Band 7+, select only the most important figures to illustrate patterns mentioned in the overview. Use comparative and superlative structures accurately (“the highest proportion”, “significantly lower than”, “roughly twice as many as”) to convey relationships between bars. Maintain coherence by deciding a clear principle for organisation (e.g., highest to lowest, or categories A–C then D–F) and sticking to it with consistent referencing. Lexically, vary your language for quantities and proportions (“just under”, “approximately”, “a minority of”, “the vast majority of”) and avoid overuse of exact numbers where approximations are more natural.
Common Mistakes to Avoid: Omitting an overview or writing one that merely repeats details rather than summarising overall patterns. Describing each bar in isolation without making comparisons, which fails the requirement to “make comparisons where relevant”. Overloading the report with all numbers from the chart, leading to poor selection and mechanical detail. Mixing up categories or units (e.g., mislabelling countries or years), which damages accuracy. Using informal language or opinion (e.g., “This country did a bad job” or “I think this is surprising”) instead of neutral reporting. Poor paragraphing, such as one single body paragraph with no clear grouping or logical progression.

Standard Report Structure: Pie charts show how a whole is divided into parts, often as percentages. The introduction paraphrases what the pies show, including time and place if given. The overview describes overall distribution and key contrasts: largest and smallest segments, major changes between years if multiple pies, and any notable shifts in proportions. Detail paragraphs then group categories (e.g., main vs minor segments) and compare proportions using specific percentages and suitable comparative language, especially when there are two or more pie charts to compare.
Tips to Achieve Band 7 and Above: Focus onrelative proportions, not raw percentages alone, and emphasise the largest and most significant changes to satisfy Task Achievement at higher bands. When there are multiple pies, compare the same category across years and highlight increases/decreases or stability, grouping similar trends to create coherent paragraphs. Use a wide range of expressions for proportions (“a quarter”, “roughly one third”, “just over half”) and avoid repeating percentage symbols in every sentence. Maintain a neutral, descriptive tone, and ensure cohesion by using referencing (“this category”, “the remaining share”) and substitution instead of repeating labels endlessly. Grammatically, show control of partitive structures and quantifiers, and use present or past tenses appropriately depending on the time frame.
Common Mistakes to Avoid: Ignoring the requirement to compare and instead describing each pie separately, which weakens coherence and Task Achievement. Failing to mention the largest or most important categories in the overview, or focusing only on minor slices. Overuse of exact percentages without any grouping, which leads to mechanical, list‑like writing. Misinterpreting the total (e.g., assuming numbers instead of percentages) and drawing incorrect comparisons. Using casual fractions or vague language (“a lot”, “many”) where more precise expressions are needed. Adding explanations or reasons for the proportions, which goes beyond the descriptive remit of Task 1.

Standard Report Structure: Tables present numerical data across categories and often over time. The introduction paraphrases what the table shows, including the variables (e.g., countries, years, items) and units. The overview highlights the main trends or comparisons: which categories are generally higher/lower, overall increases/decreases, and any striking figures. Detail paragraphs then reorganise the table into logical narrative groupings, often by row (e.g., by country) or by column (e.g., by year), selecting key figures and comparisons that support the overview instead of describing cell by cell.
Tips to Achieve Band 7 and Above: High‑band responses treat the table like a set of related bar charts: theygroup and comparerather than list all cell values. For Task Achievement, select the most relevant data to illustrate trends in the overview, choosing extremes, overall patterns, and a few representative figures. Coherence is improved by clear organisational choices (e.g., first discuss the top‑performing categories, then the lowest) and by using cohesive devices and referencing to avoid repetition. Vary your expressions for quantities and comparisons, and avoid over‑reliance on “increased/decreased” by using synonyms such as “grew”, “declined”, “remained modest”, “peaked at”. Show grammatical range by using complex sentences that combine multiple comparisons and data points accurately, while maintaining a high level of accuracy.
Common Mistakes to Avoid: Attempting to describe every cell in the table, producing a long, unfocused report that fails to highlight key features. Lacking an overview or presenting one that is just a list of numbers rather than a summary of main patterns. Confusing rows and columns or mixing up categories, which leads to inaccurate reporting. Poor paragraphing, such as switching randomly between rows and columns, which disrupts logical progression. Over‑repetition of figures and category names due to limited lexical resource and weak referencing. Using explanations or opinions about why the figures might be like that, which is outside Task 1 requirements.

Question Type: Process diagram / flow chart(how something works or is made)
Standard Report Structure: Process diagrams show the stages of a process (e.g., manufacturing, life cycle, natural process) and may include arrows, inputs, and outputs. The introduction paraphrases the diagram by stating what the process is and its general purpose or outcome. The overview summarises the overall sequence or cycle, mentioning the total number of stages, whether it is cyclic or linear, and key start and end points. Detail paragraphs then describe the stages in logical order, grouping related steps where appropriate (e.g., initial preparation vs final processing) and using appropriate sequence markers.
Tips to Achieve Band 7 and Above: For Band 7 Task Achievement, you mustcover all key stagesand highlight their sequence clearly, without omitting crucial steps or overloading minor details. Use a wide range of sequence language (“firstly”, “subsequently”, “after this”, “at the final stage”) and appropriate passive structures to describe processes objectively (“is heated”, “is transported”, “is collected”). Maintain coherence by following the diagram’s arrows from beginning to end and grouping similar steps into coherent paragraphs. Lexical resource should include technical or semi‑technical vocabulary relevant to the process where possible, while keeping meanings accurate. Grammatically, strong answers show good control of passive voice, time‑less present tense, and complex clauses that express purpose and result (“in order to”, “so that”, “which results in”).
Common Mistakes to Avoid: Missing an overview or giving only a description of individual stages without summarising the overall process. Changing the order of stages from the diagram or skipping steps, leading to an inaccurate description. Using active voice and first person (“we make”, “I then put”) inappropriately instead of neutral, process‑focused language. Adding explanations, opinions or reasons not shown in the diagram (e.g., why a company uses this method), which goes beyond the task. Overuse or incorrect use of sequence markers, making the writing sound mechanical or confusing (“after that then”, “nextly”). Frequent errors in passive structures and subject–verb agreement that reduce Grammatical Range and Accuracy scores.

Question Type: Map / plan(changes in place or layout)
Standard Report Structure: Map tasks show one or more maps of the same place at different times, or two different places/plans. The introduction paraphrases the prompt, stating what the map(s) represent and the time reference (e.g., “in 1990 and now”). The overview highlights major changes or differences: overall development, urbanisation, new facilities, removal of features, or shifts in land use. Detail paragraphs then describe changes in a logical spatial pattern (e.g., from north to south, or from centre to outskirts), comparing “before” and “after” using appropriate location and transformation language.
Tips to Achieve Band 7 and Above: To reach Band 7+, you must clearlyidentify and summarise key changesin the overview rather than describing every small alteration. Use a range of prepositions and locational phrases accurately (“to the north of”, “adjacent to”, “in the south‑western corner”) and verbs for change (“was replaced by”, “was converted into”, “was extended”). Coherence is improved by following a consistent pattern of description (e.g., left to right, centre outward) and clearly signalling when you are talking about past vs present maps. Lexical resource should include variety for describing development and land use (“residential area”, “farmland”, “recreational facilities”, “industrial zone”). Grammatically, control of past tenses and passive structures for changes over time is important, along with complex sentences comparing old and new layouts.
Common Mistakes to Avoid: Failing to provide an overview that summarises the main developments and instead listing small changes room by room or feature by feature. Mixing up directions (e.g., calling east “west”) or mislocating features, which leads to inaccurate reporting. Describing only one map and not comparing, which weakens Task Achievement. Overuse of basic prepositions (“in”, “on”) and repetition of “there is / there was”, showing limited lexical and grammatical range. Using informal or narrative style (“you can see a road here”, “this place became nicer”), which is inappropriate for an academic report. Confusing time references or tenses when describing past and present/future maps.

Standard Report Structure: Mixed questions combine two or more visuals, such as a line graph plus bar chart, or table plus pie charts. The introduction paraphrases the overall topic and mentions all visual types and time references. The overview summarises the key overall trends or relationships across visuals, such as how the chart and table relate (e.g., rising totals vs distribution), and highlights the most noticeable patterns in each. Detail paragraphs are usually organised either by visual (one paragraph per chart type) or by theme (e.g., first overall totals, then breakdowns), always making clear comparisons and connections between visuals where relevant.
Tips to Achieve Band 7 and Above: Band 7+ answers mustaddress all visualsand show how they relate, not treat them as separate, unrelated tasks. In planning, decide whether it is clearer to group by visual type or by theme, and ensure that each paragraph has a clear focus. For Task Achievement, select key figures from each visual to support an integrated overview rather than trying to cover every detail. Use a wide range of comparative language and referencing to move smoothly between visuals (“according to the bar chart…”, “in contrast, the table shows…”). Lexically, demonstrate flexibility by using appropriate vocabulary for different data types in the same report (e.g., trends over time and proportions in a breakdown). Grammatically, control of complex sentences that integrate data from both visuals in one comparison can strengthen Grammatical Range and Accuracy scores.
Common Mistakes to Avoid: Ignoring one of the visuals or giving it only a token sentence, which fails to “fully satisfy the requirements of the task”. Writing two mini‑reports with no clear connection between them, which weakens coherence and cohesion. Overloading the essay with numbers from all visuals, leading to excessive detail and poor selection of key features. Confusing data sources when referring to figures (e.g., mixing up what comes from the line graph vs the table). Weak overview that focuses only on one visual or that does not synthesise the overall picture across diagrams. Limited variety in linking phrases, causing mechanical repetition when moving between graphs (“also”, “also”, “also”).
